summaries.history: The Ravnos trace their origins to India, where according to the Karavalanisha Vrana (Wounds of the Night's Sword), they were created to hunt down the asuratizayya (countless demons)—fallen angels who had betrayed their duty. Their progenitor, Zapathasura (the Accursed Monster), was created by the gods to be a warrior against these demons. Zapathasura Embraced five childer: the Black Mother (prophetess, founder of the Brahman jati), the Rakshasa (master of deception), Chandraputra the General (founder of the Kshatriyas jati), Ravana (who bargained with demons), and Ramessu (founder of the Vaisyas jati). The clan spread from India across the world, with many traveling with the Rroma (Gypsies) into Europe. In 1999, during the Week of Nightmares, most Ravnos elders were destroyed when their Antediluvian awakened and was destroyed, leaving the clan shattered and scattered.
summaries.culture: Ravnos culture is built around individualism, freedom, and the Path of Paradox. They are wanderers who rarely stay in one place too long, valuing their independence above all else. Many Ravnos travel with nomadic groups (historically the Rroma), but they maintain their own separate identity. The clan is organized into jati (castes/lineages): Brahman (prophets and seers), Kshatriyas (warriors), Vaisyas (merchants and those who interact with mortals), and Chandalas (outcasts). Ravnos gather in kris (councils) to settle disputes and share information, but these are temporary and informal. The clan's recent devastation has left them even more isolated and paranoid.
summaries.philosophy: Ravnos philosophy centers on the Path of Paradox (mayaparisatya), which teaches that each Ravnos must find their svadharma (true purpose) by transcending their curse. The path views the Embrace as locking the soul outside the cycle of samsara (reincarnation), requiring the Ravnos to reestablish their dharma as one of the Kindred. By understanding and penetrating maya (illusion), a Ravnos can transcend the curses of undeath. The path is individualistic—each Ravnos must find their own way, and they cannot hold onto the coattails of their elders. Those who follow the path view other Kindred as inferior or dangerous if they don't seek their own svadharma.
summaries.factions: The primary divisions are the jati (castes): Brahman (prophets), Kshatriyas (warriors), Vaisyas (merchants), and Chandalas (outcasts). There are also geographical divisions: Indian Ravnos (who maintain the oldest traditions), Rroma Ravnos (who traveled with the Rroma into Europe), Western Ravnos (who have lost touch with the old ways), and Alexandrites (Middle Eastern Ravnos). Politically, Ravnos are divided between those who follow the Path of Paradox, those who follow other paths (especially in the Sabbat), and those who reject all paths. Since the Week of Nightmares, many Ravnos have gone into hiding, making factional organization even more difficult.
summaries.politics: Ravnos are largely independent, rejecting both Camarilla and Sabbat structures. They may claim membership in either sect for convenience, but they rarely commit fully. In Camarilla domains, they're often tolerated as long as they don't cause trouble, but many princes are quick to expel them. In Sabbat domains, Ravnos antitribu were once a significant presence, but the Week of Nightmares devastated their numbers. Ravnos politics are informal and based on personal relationships rather than formal hierarchies. They maintain loose networks for mutual support, especially for 'The Treatment'—coordinated harassment of princes who expel Ravnos from their domains.
summaries.relations: Ravnos have a long-standing feud with the Gangrel, dating back to conflicts over herds when Ravnos traveled with the Rroma through Europe. They're often mistaken for or confused with Malkavians due to their trickster reputation. They have a complex relationship with the Rroma (Gypsies), with many Ravnos having traveled with them historically, though the relationship is often strained. Most other clans view Ravnos with suspicion or outright hostility due to their reputation for crime and deception. Since the Week of Nightmares, many clans view the Ravnos as a dying or dead clan, which has only increased their isolation.
summaries.character_creation: Ravnos characters should emphasize survival skills, adaptability, and the ability to manipulate others. Physical Attributes are important for survival on the road, while Social Attributes support their trickster nature. Mental Attributes help with understanding the Path of Paradox and using Chimerstry effectively. Key Abilities include Stealth, Subterfuge, Streetwise, Animal Ken (for Animalism), and Performance (for creating believable illusions). Disciplines focus on Animalism, Chimerstry, and Fortitude. Backgrounds often include Contacts (in various cities), Resources (from their crimes or trades), and Retainers (animal companions or temporary allies). Each Ravnos must choose a specific type of crime they're compelled to commit.
summaries.archetypes: Common archetypes include the Wanderer who never stays in one place, the Trickster who uses Chimerstry to deceive and manipulate, the Path Seeker who follows the Path of Paradox in search of their svadharma, the Criminal who embraces their compulsion, the Survivor who escaped the Week of Nightmares, and the Rroma Traveler who maintains connections to Rroma culture. Many Ravnos archetypes center on the tension between their compulsion and their search for purpose.
summaries.storyteller_advice: Storytellers should use Ravnos to introduce elements of illusion, deception, and the blurring of reality. Their compulsion creates constant tension and moral conflict. Their Chimerstry can be used to create memorable scenes where nothing is what it seems. Remember that the Week of Nightmares has left the clan shattered—most elders are dead, and those who remain are traumatized and paranoid. Ravnos should feel like wanderers who never belong anywhere, always ready to move on. Their Path of Paradox offers a unique philosophical framework that can add depth to chronicles exploring themes of purpose, free will, and transcendence.

tone_highlights.emotional_temperature: Restless, paranoid, and darkly humorous; a constant tension between the compulsion to commit crimes and the need to transcend it.
tone_highlights.social_posture: Independent and guarded; they rarely reveal their true intentions and prefer to keep others guessing.
tone_highlights.predator_style: They use illusion, deception, and their compulsion to manipulate situations rather than direct confrontation.
recurring_motifs: Illusion and the blurring of reality
The Week of Nightmares and its aftermath
The Path of Paradox and mayaparisatya

common_archetypes.name: The Wanderer
common_archetypes.description: A Ravnos who never stays in one place too long, always moving from city to city in search of something they can't name.

common_archetypes.name: The Trickster
common_archetypes.description: A Ravnos who uses Chimerstry to deceive and manipulate, turning their curse into a tool for survival and profit.

common_archetypes.name: The Path Seeker
common_archetypes.description: A Ravnos who follows the Path of Paradox in search of their svadharma, trying to transcend their curse through understanding.

common_archetypes.name: The Criminal
common_archetypes.description: A Ravnos who embraces their compulsion, using it as a source of power and identity rather than trying to resist it.

common_archetypes.name: The Survivor
common_archetypes.description: A Ravnos who survived the Week of Nightmares, now traumatized and paranoid, struggling to understand what happened.

common_archetypes.name: The Rroma Traveler
common_archetypes.description: A Ravnos who maintains connections to Rroma culture, traveling with or near Rroma groups while maintaining their Kindred identity.
